exemplar.title:
An Experiment to Investigate the Influence of Metacognitive difficulty on Cognitive Reflection
A Partial Replication of Alter and Oppenheimer (2007)

detailedExemplarData.title:
An Experiment to Investigate the Influence of Metacognitive difficulty on Cognitive Reflection
A Partial Replication of Alter and Oppenheimer (2007)

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

files.fileName: Jamie LEE Psychology IA Report FINAL VERSION..pdf

strengths: Clearly stated and wellâjustified investigation aim with explicit relevance to realâworld cognitive demands and Alter & Oppenheimerâs work
Accurate, thorough description of the dualâprocessing model with explicit linkage to the present study
Hypotheses that correctly name and fully operationalize IV (font readability) and DV (CRT score), including directional and null formulations
weaknesses: Inconsistency in the number of CRT items (3âitem vs 5âitem) undermines clarity
Justification for choosing the Corinthia font is truncated midâsentence
Some repetitive wording in the theoretical linkage that could be tightened
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction demonstrates a precise aim, an excellent theoryâinvestigation link and fully operationalized variables, meriting placement in the 5â6 band. Minor issues (inconsistency of CRT item count, truncated font rationale and occasional repetition) prevent a topâband score of 6, so a 5 is justified.

strengths: Research design is not only described but explained (avoidance of order effects via independent measures)
Sampling technique is specified and its implications for generalisability are discussed
Controlled variables and materials are listed with reasons for their use to support internal validity
weaknesses: No justification of sample size or power analysis to support adequacy of comparisons
Reliance on a convenience, schoolâbased sample limits external validity
Number of participants per condition is not specified, reducing methodological transparency
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands meet the âexplainedâ descriptor, placing the exploration squarely in the top band (3â4). There are no substantive omissions that would warrant a deduction below the full mark, so it receives a 4.

strengths: Appropriate choice of descriptive statistics and MannâWhitney U test
Graphical presentation (bar chart with SD error bars) effectively addresses the hypothesis
Findings are clearly linked back to the hypothesis and model, rejecting the null in line with predictions
weaknesses: Internal inconsistencies in Uâvalue reporting and confusion over critical values
Figure caption is unclear and isolated from the rest of the title
Lack of normality checks, effectâsize reporting and confidence intervals limits depth of analysis
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the graphing and interpretation strands meet topâband standards, significant errors and inconsistencies in the statistical analysis strand place it in the 3â4 band. Weighing these together, a holistic score of 4 of 6 accurately reflects the mixed highâlow performance.

strengths: Findings are related to the dualâprocessing model and metacognitive fluency
Strengths and limitations are explained with direct relevance to validity and generalisability
Modifications are explicitly linked to identified limitations and fully justified
weaknesses: Discussion of findings is brief and would benefit from deeper engagement with alternative theories
Ethical considerations and rationale for procedural changes (e.g., time limit adjustments) are not addressed
Limited exploration of boundary conditions or potential counterarguments
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation demonstrates good linkage to theory, relevant strengths/limitations and justified modifications, placing most strands in the 5â6 band. However, brevity in the theoretical discussion and missing ethical/procedural rationale limit it to the 3â4 band, justifying a score of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar chart clearly contrasts means for each condition, includes error bars (SD) and labels that correspond directly to the hypothesis. Minor aesthetic issues aside, the technique is correct and hypothesis-focused, satisfying the top-band descriptor; a mid-band score of 5 is appropriate given slight labelling limitations.

The student states the statistical outcome, rejects the null, and **explicitly links** higher difficult-font scores to the directional hypothesis and to deeper System 2 thinking, demonstrating clear interpretation.  As the interpretation is correct and connected to both data and hypothesis, the work meets the top-band criterion; moderate depth justifies a 5.

Both IV and DV are not only named but **fully operationalised**: IV = font readability (Arial 12-pt vs Corinthia 20-pt); DV = number of correct answers on a five-item CRT.  Directional and null hypotheses are written correctly with statistical directionality noted.  This matches the highest descriptor and merits the top of the band.

Convenience sampling is described and its implications for generalisability are **explicitly discussed**, together with an explanation of random allocation within the sample.  Such justification fulfils the âexplainedâ criterion, so the work earns the higher mark in the 3-4 band.

The dual-processing model is **accurately and thoroughly described**, outlining the features of System 1 and System 2 with strengths and limitations.  The student then **explicitly links** this theory to the present investigation, arguing that a disfluent font should trigger System 2 and lead to higher CRT scores.  Because both description and linkage are present and largely error-free, the work sits securely in the top band.

Findings are related to the dual-processing model and metacognitive fluency, but this linkage is **brief** and lacks a nuanced discussion of alternative explanations or theoretical implications.  Therefore, the strand achieves the 3-4 band, with the higher mark awarded because reference to background theory is present.

The student clearly states a precise aim â to examine whether font-induced metacognitive difficulty affects performance on a 5-item CRT â and then **explains its relevance** through reference to real-world cognitive demands, the original Alter & Oppenheimer findings and the desire to extend ecological validity.  The commentary moves beyond a simple identification by justifying why each methodological change serves the overall aim, demonstrating an explicit explanation of relevance, which best fits the 5-6 band.

Several strengths and limitations are not only identified but **explained in terms of their impact on validity and generalisability** (e.g., order effects, homogeneity, uncontrolled individual differences).  Explanations are clear and directly connected to the investigation, satisfying the 5-6 descriptor; minor omissions (e.g., ethical issues) suggest a 5 rather than 6.

Controlled variables (time limit, layout, demographic homogeneity) and materials (handouts, randomiser, instructions, consent, debrief) are listed **and reasons for their use are provided**, satisfying the requirement for explanation rather than mere description.  Hence the strand sits at the top of the 3-4 range.

Proposed improvements (matched-pairs design, stratified sampling, varied time limits, practice trials) are **explicitly tied to the corresponding limitations** and justified in terms of enhancing control or reliability.  This direct linkage and justification fit the top band; depth is good but not exhaustive, so a score of 5 is appropriate.

The student does more than list an independent-measures design; they **explain why** it was chosen (avoid order effects) and mention random assignment, thereby addressing purpose and suitability.  This explanatory quality places the strand in the 3-4 band, warranting the upper mark.

Appropriate descriptive statistics and a Mann-Whitney U are chosen, but numerical reporting is **internally inconsistent** (two different U values appear, and critical value logic is confused).  These calculation/ reporting errors mean application is not fully accurate, matching the 3-4 descriptor; the lower mark in that band reflects the noticeable mistakes.

annotations.comment: Consider tightening the explanation by distinguishing more explicitly between the automatic heuristics of System 1 and the resourceâintensive operations of System 2, avoiding repetitive wording.
The linkage to Alter & Oppenheimer (2007) in the background section effectively sets context by situating the study within its original theoretical framework.
The student provides a clear and concise description of the dualâprocessing model, correctly outlining System 1 and System 2 characteristics with minimal error.
The aim and operationalisation of IV and DV are explicit, naming font readability conditions and correct CRT responses, demonstrating strong hypothesis formation.
The dependent variable is described using a 3âitem CRT, yet elsewhere a 5âitem CRT is used. Resolve this inconsistency to maintain clarity and accuracy.
Consider extending the hypothesis section by explaining why using a novel set of CRT items enhances generalisability, linking it back to ecological validity.
The investigation paragraph ends midâsentence, truncating the justification for choosing Corinthia. Ensure the full rationale for this font manipulation is presented.
Linking findings back to the dual-processing model is effective, but consider discussing why font disfluency may not perfectly map onto System 2 activation.
The inline bullet formatting for hypothesis clarification appears awkward. Use a simple parenthetical statement or plain text for readability.
The independentâmeasures design is well explained with rationale for random assignment and orderâeffect avoidance, satisfying an âexplainedâ descriptor.
It would strengthen the design section to include a justification of sample size or power analysis, ensuring adequacy of group comparisons.
Convenience sampling is described and its implications for generalisability are discussed, demonstrating explanation of sampling choices.
While demographic homogeneity controls variance, rely solely on a school-based sample limits external validity; discuss this sampling bias more fully.
In the controlled variables section, further explain how each control (e.g., time limit, layout) isolates font readability effects to reinforce internal validity.
Specify the number of participants allocated per condition when describing choice of materials and randomisation for clearer methodology reporting.
The rationale for using mean and standard deviation is clearly articulated, demonstrating understanding of descriptive statisticsâ purposes.
Avoid implying statistical significance when describing means alone. Reserve such claims until inferential results are presented.
The descriptive statistics table is wellâlabelled with clear headings and values matching the text, aiding comprehension.
Consider adding confidence intervals for each mean to provide additional insight into the precision of the estimates.
Figure 1 is effectively designed with means and SD error bars, directly addressing the central hypothesis.
The caption âIndependent variableâ is isolated and unclear; integrate it into the figure title or expand it to describe both conditions for clarity.
There is inconsistency between the Uâvalue reported here (45.5) and the appendix (243.5). Clarify which value is correct and reconcile the discrepancy.
Justify the choice of the MannâWhitney U test by reporting tests of normality or distribution checks for completeness.
Report an effect size (e.g., r or Î·Â²) alongside significance to convey the practical magnitude of the font readability effect.
The findings discussion is concise but could benefit from deeper engagement with alternative theoretical accounts or boundary conditions.
The evaluation of the independentâmeasures design clearly links to orderâeffect control and suggests a matchedâpairs alternative, demonstrating strong evaluation.
The discussion of convenience sampling is thorough, noting selection bias and suggesting stratified sampling to enhance generalisability.
The procedure evaluation mentions an initial 10-second limit but doesnât explain why it was changed; clarify this rationale to support your modifications.
In the evaluation, a brief ethical considerations section (e.g., data handling, debrief efficacy) would strengthen alignment with ethical research practice.
